```{r}
  Coefficient = as.numeric(coef_values_int),
  Abs_Coefficient = abs(as.numeric(coef_values_int)),
  Odds_Ratio = exp(Coefficient)
) %>%
  arrange(desc(Abs_Coefficient)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)

knitr::kable(
  coef_table,
  digits = 3,
  col.names = c("Predictor", "Coefficient", "Absolute Coefficient", "Odds Ratio"),
  caption = "Table 3. Top 10 logistic regression coefficients by absolute value for the full interaction model."
)
```

```{r fig.cap="Figure 13. Logistic regression variable importance."}
p3 <- vip::vip(log_full_interaction, num_features = length(log_full_interaction$variable.importance), bar = FALSE)
p4 <- vip::vip(log_reduced_interaction, num_features = length(log_reduced_interaction$variable.importance), bar = FALSE)

p3 <- p3 + ggtitle("7.3.1 LR - Full Dataset")
p4 <- p4 + ggtitle("7.3.2 LR - Reduced Dataset")

vip_lr <- grid.arrange(p3, p4, nrow = 1)
```

```{r}
ggsave(
  "fig13-VIP-LR.png",
  plot = vip_lr,
  width = 12,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 300
)
```

```{r fig.cap="Figure 14. Random forest variable importance."}
p1 <- vip::vip(rf1_tuned, num_features = length(rf1_tuned$variable.importance), bar = FALSE)
p2 <- vip::vip(rf2_tuned, num_features = length(rf2_tuned$variable.importance), bar = FALSE)

p1 <- p1 + ggtitle("7.3.3 RF - Full Dataset")
p2 <- p2 + ggtitle("7.3.4 RF - Reduced Dataset")

vip_rf <- grid.arrange(p1, p2, nrow = 1)
```

```{r}
ggsave(
  "fig14-VIP-RF.png",
  plot = vip_rf,
  width = 12,
  height = 6,
  dpi = 300
)
```

### 8.1 Most Important Predictors

In Table 3, patients with four or more pre-existing conditions had 5.84 times the odds of adverse outcome compared with patients with no recorded conditions. The odds increased with condition count, from about 2.0 times higher for one condition to 4.32 times higher for three conditions.

Chronic renal disease had an odds ratio of approximately 2.0, similar to having one recorded condition. Male patients had 1.87 times the odds of adverse outcome compared with female patients. Tobacco use and asthma had odds ratios around 0.5, suggesting lower estimated odds in this model, though these should not be interpreted as protective without further clinical validation.

Variable importance showed similar patterns across models: age ranked highest, followed by condition count, diabetes, sex, and hypertension. Chronic renal disease had the highest random forest importance among near-zero variance features, consistent with the association rule results.

### 8.2 Interaction Patterns

The logistic regression interaction models provided a formal evaluation of selected interaction terms, but they did not substantially improve performance compared with the non-interaction models. This suggests that most predictive signal was captured by main effects, especially age, condition count, diabetes, hypertension, sex, and chronic renal disease.

Random forest likely captured some nonlinear and interaction patterns internally, but these effects are less directly visible than logistic regression coefficients. Therefore, interpretation relied on combined evidence from odds ratios, variable importance, and association rule patterns.

## 9. Limitations

Sampling bias: This dataset includes confirmed positive COVID-19 cases identified through medical units, so individuals who were sick but never tested are not represented. This may underestimate total infections and bias the sample toward more severe cases or patients with greater healthcare access.

Limited generalizability: The dataset comes from the first year of the pandemic, before widespread vaccination and later COVID-19 variants. As a result, the findings may not fully apply to later pandemic periods, vaccinated populations, or newer variants. In addition, the data were collected from medical units in Mexico, so the sample may not represent the general population or patients in other healthcare systems.

Class imbalance: The target variable represented approximately 15% of observations. Hybrid sampling was applied only to the training data, while the test set retained the original class distribution. Because of this imbalance, sensitivity, F1-score, and AUC were emphasized over accuracy.

Measurement error: Several conditions may have been self-reported or recorded during medical evaluation, which could cause underreporting of some pre-existing conditions. Some patients may also have been sent home but later experienced an adverse outcome, depending on follow-up timing.

Missing variables: The Kaggle dataset included a limited set of demographic and pre-existing condition variables. Other relevant conditions, such as cancer or mental health disorders, were not available. The dataset also lacked clinical measurements and laboratory values that were available in the original government data. Including these variables would likely improve predictive performance.

Overfitting: Overfitting did not appear to be a major issue because model performance was similar across versions and remained moderate overall. However, oversampling can increase overfitting risk, so performance was evaluated on the unchanged test set.

## 10. Conclusions and Recommendations

Age was the strongest factor associated with severe COVID-19 outcomes. Risk increased further when older age was combined with multiple pre-existing conditions. Chronic renal disease was also one of the most important predictors, showing consistently high lift in association rule mining and an elevated odds ratio in logistic regression. Consistent with the literature review, diabetes and hypertension were among the most prevalent conditions in the dataset. However, the results also showed that condition burden itself was important, as the odds of adverse outcome increased as the number of pre-existing conditions increased.

For prediction, random forest performed better than logistic regression at identifying adverse outcome cases, with higher sensitivity and F1-score. Logistic regression remained more interpretable, but its low sensitivity limited its usefulness for detecting the minority class. The overall predictive performance was likely limited by the available variables, since this Kaggle dataset did not include laboratory values or detailed clinical measurements that were important in prior studies.

These findings suggest that patients with chronic renal disease, multiple pre-existing conditions, and older age may require closer monitoring during severe respiratory outbreaks. Future research should use more current data that include vaccination status, newer COVID-19 variants, and clinical measurements to determine whether these risk patterns remain consistent in later pandemic periods.
